title: Identifying SE-Specific Cell States via Leave-One-Sample-Out Cross-Validation
text: identify cell states specifically enriched SE conserved across discovery samples, can perform leave-one-sample-cross-validation (LOOCV). procedure builds upon NMF Model Development Spatial Ecotype Recovery Single-Cell Spatial Transcriptomics Data. LOOCV iteration, NMF model trained using one sample applied predict cell state labels held-sample. enrichment cell state within SE quantified based ability correctly assign cells SE, measured F1 score. cell state considered SE-specific 1) F1 score assigned SE highest across SEs; 2) difference top F1 score second-highest F1 score exceeds predefined threshold. tutorial, demonstrate procedure using two samples profiled Vizgen MERSCOPE. title: Identifying SE-Specific Cell States via Leave-One-Sample-Out Cross-Validation
text: SpatialEcoTyper defines SEs based spatial co-variation cell states across cell types samples. However, cell states associated SE necessarily specific SE. identify SE-specific conserved cell states, perform LOOCV iteratively: 1) Training NMF models one sample; 2) Predicting SE assignments held-sample. LoocvPredict function performs procedure returns predicted SE labels cells. Note: default parameters LoocvPredict optimized pan-cancer SEs (Zhang et al., Nature 2026). user-defined datasets, parameter tuning recommended: scale: apply unit-variance normalization balance.sample: balance cell numbers across samples nfeature: number variable genes per cell type nfeature.per.se: number genes retained per SE downsample: number cells used training repeats: number model training iterations (robustness)
code: loocv_preds = LoocvPredict(scdata = scdata, scmeta = scmeta,                             Sample = "Sample", SE = "SE",                             CellType = "CellType",                             repeats = 1, ncores = 16)

title: Identifying SE-Specific Cell States via Leave-One-Sample-Out Cross-Validation
text: LOOCV, compare original SE assignments predicted labels. ComputeMetrics function computes pairwise metrics (F1, F2, precision, recall) across SE-associated cell states. define specificity based : Maximum F1 score corresponds assigned SE Difference top two F1 scores exceeds 0.1 Cell states meet criteria considered SE-specific conserved across samples. Cell states meet criteria broadly distributed across multiple SEs, conserved across samples. states excluded SE-specific analyses may still represent biologically meaningful programs shared across microenvironments.  Note tutorial uses two small datasets demonstration purposes . result, identified spatial ecotypes (SEs) cell states may generalize due limited sample size. recommend applying workflow larger, well-powered datasets validating findings using independent held-data. model training involves stochastic subsampling cells, recommend repeating LOOCV procedure multiple times different random seeds ensure robustness. repetition, compute performance metrics (e.g., F1 scores) average across runs. Finally, NMF model training computationally intensive, full LOOCV procedure can time-consuming. improve efficiency, recommend running training jobs Slurm array jobs allocating sufficient computational resources (e.g., CPU cores) task. example Slurm array script provided . run_loocv_pred.R submit_slurm_jobs_run_loocv_pred.sh
code: ## Compute F1 matrix  F1s = ComputeMetrics(loocv_preds, SE = "SE", Pred = "cvPred",                       CellType = "CellType", Sample = "Sample", metric = "F1") ## Filter for the specific cell states and visualize the F1 matrix delta <- apply(F1s, 1, function(x) {   x <- sort(x, decreasing = TRUE)   x[1] - x[2] }) idx = delta>0.1 & apply(F1s, 1, which.max)==1:nrow(F1s) ## Criteria for specificity gg = F1s[idx, idx] idx = sort(rownames(gg)) gg = gg[idx, idx] HeatmapView(gg, c(0, 0.1, 0.3)) SE_cellstates = rownames(gg) SE_cellstates ## SE-specific cell states ##  [1] "SE3_CD4T"        "SE3_CD8T"        "SE3_DC"          "SE3_Endothelial" ##  [5] "SE3_Fibroblast"  "SE3_Macrophage"  "SE3_NK"          "SE4_CD4T"        ##  [9] "SE4_CD8T"        "SE4_DC"          "SE7_CD8T"        "SE7_DC"          ## [13] "SE7_Endothelial" "SE7_Fibroblast"  "SE7_Macrophage"  "SE7_Plasma"      ## [17] "SE8_Endothelial" # ============================================================ # Demo script for LOOCV analysis using LoocvPredict (do not run as-is without setup) # Designed for Slurm array jobs: one run per task # ============================================================  # ---- Parse command-line argument (task index) ---- args <- commandArgs(trailingOnly = TRUE)  if (length(args) < 1) {   stop("Usage: Rscript run_loocv_pred.R <task_id>") }  i <- as.integer(args[1]) if (is.na(i)) {   stop("Error: <task_id> must be an integer.") }  message("LOOCV iteration: ", i)  # ---- Load required libraries and data (user-defined) ---- library(SpatialEcoTyper) # scdata <- ... # scmeta <- ...  # ---- Run ---- loocv_preds = LoocvPredict(scdata = scdata, scmeta = scmeta,                             Sample = "Sample", SE = "SE",                             CellType = "CellType",                             repeats = 1, ncores = 32) # ---- Save output ---- saveRDS(loocv_preds, paste0("path/to/SE_loocv_preds_", i, ".rds"))  F1s = ComputeMetrics(loocv_preds, SE = "SE", Pred = "cvPred",                       CellType = "CellType", Sample = "Sample", metric = "F1") saveRDS(F1s, paste0("path/to/SE_loocv_preds_", i, "_F1_matrices.rds")) #!/bin/bash #SBATCH --job-name=nmf_train #SBATCH --array=1-30 #SBATCH --cpus-per-task=32 #SBATCH --mem=32G #SBATCH --time=24:00:00 #SBATCH --output=logs/nmf_%A_%a.out Rscript run_loocv_pred.R $SLURM_ARRAY_TASK_ID

Check Tutorial 1 details. Identification conserved spatial ecotypes (Steps 5-9): Spatial neighborhood clusters discovered individual single-cell ST samples represented gene expression profiles (GEPs) associated cell states, clusters similar GEPs aggregated across samples identify conserved SEs. title: Discovering Conserved Spatial Ecotypes Across Multiple Spatial Transcriptomics Samples
text: IntegrateSpatialEcoTyper function provides convenient way integrate Spatial EcoTyper results across samples cancer types identify conserved spatial ecotypes. function assumes SpatialEcoTyper run sample using consistent settings. proceeding, please review full manual IntegrateSpatialEcoTyper function . Choosing louvain clustering resolution: Louvain clustering applied group spatial neighborhoods clusters within sample, improves computational efficiency integrative analysis samples. recommend using high clustering resolution feasible (30 higher) better preserve fine-grained biological structure ensure robustness parameter variation. Choosing optimal number SEs: NMF clustering used identify conserved spatial ecotypes across samples. determine optimal number SEs, users can specify nmf_ranks nrun.per.rank arguments evaluate multiple candidate ranks, defined number runs (recommend ≥30 runs per rank ensure robustness, NMF uses random initialization). rank, cophenetic coefficient computed assess clustering stability. metric ranges 0 1, higher values indicating greater stability. Typically, cophenetic coefficient exhibits multi-modal pattern across ranks, selected optimal number SEs highest rank cophenetic coefficient exceeds 0.95 (controlled min.coph argument) followed largest subsequent decrease, indicating transition less stable solutions. Balancing predefined regions: predefined tissue regions available (e.g., pathologist annotations tumor adjacent stromal regions), can use Region downsample..region argument balance spatial neighborhoods across regions. enabled, method downsamples neighborhoods within region ensure comparable representation weight regions equally integration.

title: Discovering Conserved Spatial Ecotypes Across Multiple Spatial Transcriptomics Samples
text: MultiSpatialEcoTyper function provides end--end pipeline integrative analysis single-cell spatial transcriptomics data across multiple samples. streamlined workflow convenient, can computationally intensive, single-sample Spatial EcoTyper analysis memory- time-demanding step. Therefore, recommend first analyzing sample individually using SpatialEcoTyper function, demonstrated Tutorial 1. resulting outputs can integrated across samples using IntegrateSpatialEcoTyper function, described . First, load single-cell gene expression data meta data. , can submit job wait. similarity network fusion (Step 2 SpatialEcoTyper) computationally demanding step, runtime increases number spatial neighborhoods (SNs) per sample. example, analysis involving 10 cell types 18,564 SNs required 436 minutes runtime reached peak memory usage 169 GB. benchmark obtained computing cluster equipped AMD EPYC 7543 processor (2.75 GHz) 256 GB RAM, using single CPU core excluding data loading time. reduce runtime, users can increase number cores via ncores parameter. However, parallelization comes cost increased memory consumption. memory resources limited, users can reduce number spatial neighborhoods increasing grid.size, makes spatial grid coarser. Increasing radius can also reduce number unique SNs, changes spatial resolution used carefully.

what: Identifying a different number of spatial ecotypes
title: Discovering Conserved Spatial Ecotypes Across Multiple Spatial Transcriptomics Samples
text: SpatialEcoTyper uses NMF group spatial clusters multiple samples conserved SEs. want try different number clusters spatial ecotype identification, can use nmfClustering function. analysis requires fused similarity network matrix (MultiSE_integrated_final.rds) generated previous step. instance, want identify 10 SEs, can specific ranks = 10 identify 10 clusters. determine optimal number clusters, can provide multiple candidate values ranks argument nmfClustering function, evaluates specified rank computes corresponding cophenetic coefficient assess clustering stability. Running time step time-consuming, time requirement dependent data size, number ranks test, number runs per rank, number cores parallel analysis. optimal rank determined, can use generate final SE annotations.
code: ## Fused similarity matrix of spatial clusters across all samples outdir = "SpatialEcoTyper_results/" integrated = readRDS(file.path(outdir, "MultiSE_integrated_final.rds")) dim(integrated) ## [1] 385 385 ## Single-cell metadata with an 'SE' column annotating the discovered SEs # Column InitSE: Sample-level spatial clusters (before integration) # Column SE: Conserved spatial ecotypes (after cross-sample integration) finalmeta = readRDS(file.path(outdir, "MultiSE_metadata_final.rds")) head(finalmeta[, c("Sample", "X", "Y", "CellType", "Region", "InitSE", "SE")]) ##                                  Sample        X         Y   CellType Region ## HumanMelanomaPatient1__cell_3655   SKCM 1894.706 -6367.766 Fibroblast Stroma ## HumanMelanomaPatient1__cell_3657   SKCM 1942.480 -6369.602 Fibroblast Stroma ## HumanMelanomaPatient1__cell_3658   SKCM 1963.007 -6374.026 Fibroblast Stroma ## HumanMelanomaPatient1__cell_3660   SKCM 1981.600 -6372.266 Fibroblast Stroma ## HumanMelanomaPatient1__cell_3661   SKCM 1742.939 -6374.851 Fibroblast Stroma ## HumanMelanomaPatient1__cell_3663   SKCM 1921.683 -6383.309 Fibroblast Stroma ##                                           InitSE  SE ## HumanMelanomaPatient1__cell_3655  SKCM..InitSE69 SE7 ## HumanMelanomaPatient1__cell_3657 SKCM..InitSE146 SE1 ## HumanMelanomaPatient1__cell_3658 SKCM..InitSE146 SE1 ## HumanMelanomaPatient1__cell_3660 SKCM..InitSE146 SE1 ## HumanMelanomaPatient1__cell_3661  SKCM..InitSE92 SE7 ## HumanMelanomaPatient1__cell_3663  SKCM..InitSE69 SE7 nmf_res <- nmfClustering(integrated, ranks = 10, nrun.per.rank = 30,                           seed = 1, ncores = 8) ses <- predict(nmf_res)  ## Add the newly defined SEs into the meta data finalmeta$SE = paste0("SE", ses[finalmeta$InitSE]) head(finalmeta) nmf_res <- nmfClustering(integrated, ranks = 2:30,                          nrun.per.rank = 30, # Recommend 30 or higher                          min.coph = 0.95,                          ncores = 4,                          seed = 1) paste0("The selected rank is ", nmf_res$bestK) ## Visualize the change of cophenetic coefficient across ranks plot(nmf_res$p) ## Extract new clustering results ses <- predict(nmf_res$NMFfits[[paste0("K.", nmf_res$bestK)]])  ## Add the newly defined SEs into the meta data finalmeta$SE = paste0("SE", ses[finalmeta$InitSE]) head(finalmeta)

Users can create NMF model deconvolve SEs bulk gene expression data. training data can derived pseudo-bulk mixtures created aggregating single-cell transcriptomics data. raw count sample available scRNAseq_demo_counts.tsv SE recovery results available scRNAseq_demo_RecoveredSEs.tsv. Loading data Note: demonstration purposes, used 1,337 cells grouped SEs create pseudo-bulk mixtures. limited cell number offers basic example, may fully capture diverse characteristics SEs, potentially affecting robustness model training subsequent SE recovery. practice, recommend using comprehensive dataset accurately reflects properties SEs, ensuring training process results reliable recovery model. Creating pseudobulk data CreatePseudobulks function used create pseudo-bulk mixtures single-cell (spatial) transcriptomics data cells grouped SEs. samples cell fractions Gaussian distribution, sets negative fractions 0, re-normalizes fractions sum 1 across SEs. Based resulting fractions, samples 1,000 cells per pseudo-bulk mixture replacement, aggregates transcriptomes non-log linear space, normalizes resulting mixture log CPM using Seurat’s NormalizeData. Training NMFGenerateW function used train NMF model SE deconvolution based provided SE fractions gene expression matrix. applying NMF, gene’s expression scaled mean 0 unit variance (recommended). meet non-negativity requirement NMF, expression matrix transformed using posneg method. transformation splits expression matrix two matrices: one containing positive values containing absolute values negative values. two matrices concatenated form final training data NMF model. resulting W matrix can used infer SE abundances bulk gene expression profiles using DeconvoluteSE function. Deconvolution Users can train NMF model SE deconvolution following tutorial . , new model can used SE deconvolution specifying W parameter:
code: # Load single-cell gene expression matrix. counts = fread("https://spatialecotyper.stanford.edu/inc/inc.public.vignettes.php?file=scRNAseq_demo_counts.tsv", sep = "\t", data.table = FALSE) rownames(counts) = counts[, 1] ## Set the first column as row names counts = counts[, -1] ## Drop the first column head(counts[, 1:5])  # Load SE recovery results SEs = read.table("https://spatialecotyper.stanford.edu/inc/inc.public.vignettes.php?file=scRNAseq_demo_RecoveredSEs.tsv", sep = "\t", row.names = 1, header = TRUE) SEs = SEs[match(colnames(counts), rownames(SEs)), 1] # extract SE predictions length(SEs) table(SEs) result = CreatePseudobulks(counts = counts, groups = SEs, n_mixtures = 100) head(result$Mixtures[, 1:5]) ## Gene expression matrix of pseudobulks head(result$Fracs) ## SE fractions in pseudobulks W = NMFGenerateW(t(result$Fracs), result$Mixtures) ## This step should be done within 1 min head(W) sefracs <- DeconvoluteSE(bulkdata, W = W)

text: RecoverSE function used assign single cells SEs. Users can either use default models recover predefined SEs use custom models recover newly defined SEs. Note: recover SEs single-cell data, must specify either celltypes se_results RecoverSE function. neither provided, assume input data represents spot-level spatial transcriptomics, SE abundances inferred directly spot. default NMF models trained discovery MERSCOPE data, encompassing five cancer types: melanoma, four carcinomas. models tailored nine distinct cell types: B cells, CD4+ T cells, CD8+ T cells, NK cells, plasma cells, macrophages, dendritic cells, fibroblasts, endothelial cells. model facilitates recovery SEs single-cell datasets, allowing cell-type-specific SE analysis. Thus, SE recovery using default models, cells query data grouped one “B”, “CD4T”, “CD8T”, “NK”, “Plasma”, “Macrophage”, “DC”, “Fibroblast”, “Endothelial”, case sensitive. cell types considered non-SE compartments. SE recovery, recommend use unified embedding spatial microregions performing de novo Spatial EcoTyper analysis, integrate gene expression spatial information. embedding enhance accuracy refinement SE recovery results. detailed tutorial Spatial EcoTyper analysis available Discovery Spatial Ecotypes Single-cell Spatial Dataset. demonstration purposes, used SpatialEcoTyper group cells spatial clusters resolution 10. practice, recommend experimenting multiple resolutions ensure robust reliable results. specify se_results RecoverSE function SE recovery. can also recover SEs without using spatial embedding, less accurate due lack spatial information. cell type annotations required case. Cells grouped one “B”, “CD4T”, “CD8T”, “NK”, “Plasma”, “Macrophage”, “DC”, “Fibroblast”, “Endothelial”, case sensitive. cell types considered non-SE compartments. use custom models, users first develop recovery models following tutorial Development SE Recovery Models. resulting models can used SE recovery. example model available SE_Recovery_W_list.rds. Using custom models specifying Ws argument:
code: ## make sure the cells are grouped into one of "B", "CD4T", "CD8T", "NK", "Plasma", "Macrophage", "DC", "Fibroblast", and "Endothelial". print(unique(scmeta$CellType)) ## [1] "Fibroblast"  "Endothelial" "DC"          "Macrophage"  "CD8T"        ## [6] "CD4T"        "Plasma"      "NK"          "B" ## Spatial EcoTyper analysis: it would take ~2 min emb = SpatialEcoTyper(normdata, scmeta, resolution = 10) emb$obj ## A seurat object including the spatial embedding ## An object of class Seurat  ## 2315 features across 2315 samples within 1 assay  ## Active assay: RNA (2315 features, 2315 variable features) ##  3 layers present: counts, data, scale.data ##  2 dimensional reductions calculated: pca, umap head(emb$metadata[, c("X", "Y", "CellType", "SE")])  ## Single-cell meta data with SE annotations ##                                         X         Y   CellType   SE ## HumanMelanomaPatient1__cell_3655 1894.706 -6367.766 Fibroblast SE25 ## HumanMelanomaPatient1__cell_3657 1942.480 -6369.602 Fibroblast SE29 ## HumanMelanomaPatient1__cell_3658 1963.007 -6374.026 Fibroblast SE29 ## HumanMelanomaPatient1__cell_3660 1981.600 -6372.266 Fibroblast SE29 ## HumanMelanomaPatient1__cell_3661 1742.939 -6374.851 Fibroblast SE48 ## HumanMelanomaPatient1__cell_3663 1921.683 -6383.309 Fibroblast SE25 sepreds <- RecoverSE(normdata, se_results = emb) head(sepreds) ## HumanMelanomaPatient1__cell_3655 HumanMelanomaPatient1__cell_3657  ##                            "SE1"                            "SE4"  ## HumanMelanomaPatient1__cell_3658 HumanMelanomaPatient1__cell_3660  ##                            "SE4"                            "SE4"  ## HumanMelanomaPatient1__cell_3661 HumanMelanomaPatient1__cell_3663  ##                            "SE3"                            "SE1" ## make sure the cells are grouped into one of "B", "CD4T", "CD8T", "NK", "Plasma", "Macrophage", "DC", "Fibroblast", and "Endothelial". print(unique(scmeta$CellType)) sepreds <- RecoverSE(normdata, celltypes = scmeta$CellType) head(sepreds) # Download SE recovery model drive_download(as_id("171WaAe49babYB85Cn1FcoNNE-lzYp1T_"), "SE_Recovery_W_list.rds", overwrite = TRUE) Ws <- readRDS("SE_Recovery_W_list.rds") ## make sure the cell type names are consistent. print(unique(scmeta$CellType)) print(names(Ws)) sepreds <- RecoverSE(normdata, celltypes = scmeta$CellType, Ws = Ws)  ## If Spatial EcoTyper result is available, we recommend: sepreds <- RecoverSE(normdata, se_results = emb, Ws = Ws)

title: Recovering Spatial Ecotypes from Single-Cell RNA-seq Data
text: tutorial, illustrate recover spatial ecotypes (SEs) single-cell RNA-seq. First load required packages vignette
code: suppressPackageStartupMessages(library(dplyr)) suppressPackageStartupMessages(library(ggplot2)) suppressPackageStartupMessages(library(parallel)) suppressPackageStartupMessages(library(Seurat)) suppressPackageStartupMessages(library(data.table)) library(SpatialEcoTyper)

title: Recovering Spatial Ecotypes from Single-Cell RNA-seq Data
text: recovery process requires normalized gene expression matrix vector cell type annotations. seurat object demo data can accessed scRNAseq_demo_seurat_obj.rds. First, load seurat object R: Next, normalize gene expression data using SCTransform NormalizeData. , use NormalizeData demonstration. , extract normalized gene expression matrix cell type annotations Seurat object. Sparse matrix .mtx format can imported using ReadMtx function Seurat package. Tab-delimited files can loaded R using fread function data.table package. TSV file expression matrix can accessed scRNAseq_demo_counts.tsv cell type annotations available scRNAseq_demo_celltype_ann.tsv.
code: url <- "https://spatialecotyper.stanford.edu/inc/inc.public.vignettes.php?file=scRNAseq_demo_seurat_obj.rds" download.file(url, destfile = "scRNAseq_demo_seurat_obj.rds", mode = "wb") # Load the seurat object obj <- readRDS("scRNAseq_demo_seurat_obj.rds") obj ## An object of class Seurat  ## 27425 features across 1337 samples within 1 assay  ## Active assay: RNA (27425 features, 2000 variable features) ##  3 layers present: counts, data, scale.data ##  2 dimensional reductions calculated: pca, umap # Cell type annotations unique(obj$CellType) ## [1] "Macrophage" "B"          "CD4T"       "CD8T"       "Other"      ## [6] "Fibroblast" "Epithelial" "Plasma" obj <- NormalizeData(obj, verbose = FALSE) normdata <- GetAssayData(obj, layer = "data") head(normdata[, 1:5]) ## 6 x 5 sparse Matrix of class "dgCMatrix" ##            AAACCTGCACATGACT-1 AAACCTGGTGGTCCGT-1 AAACCTGGTTTGTGTG-1 ## AL627309.1                  .                  .                  . ## AL627309.5                  .                  .                  . ## AP006222.2                  .                  .                  . ## AC114498.1                  .                  .                  . ## AL669831.2                  .                  .                  . ## LINC01409                   .                  .                  . ##            AAACCTGTCCGATATG-1 AAACCTGTCTAACGGT-1 ## AL627309.1          .                          . ## AL627309.5          0.2951209                  . ## AP006222.2          .                          . ## AC114498.1          .                          . ## AL669831.2          .                          . ## LINC01409           .                          . ctann = obj$CellType head(ctann) ## AAACCTGCACATGACT-1 AAACCTGGTGGTCCGT-1 AAACCTGGTTTGTGTG-1 AAACCTGTCCGATATG-1  ##       "Macrophage"                "B"             "CD4T"       "Macrophage"  ## AAACCTGTCTAACGGT-1 AAACGGGCACTTAAGC-1  ##                "B"             "CD8T" table(ctann) ## ctann ##          B       CD4T       CD8T Epithelial Fibroblast Macrophage      Other  ##         97        138        425        121         44        374         91  ##     Plasma  ##         47 ## Load the gene expression matrix scdata = ReadMtx(mtx = "https://spatialecotyper.stanford.edu/inc/inc.public.vignettes.php?file=scRNAseq_demo_matrix.mtx", features = "https://spatialecotyper.stanford.edu/inc/inc.public.vignettes.php?file=scRNAseq_demo_features.tsv",                   cells = "https://spatialecotyper.stanford.edu/inc/inc.public.vignettes.php?file=scRNAseq_demo_barcodes.tsv", feature.column = 1, cell.column = 1)  ## Normalize the data normdata = NormalizeData(scdata) head(normdata[, 1:5]) ## Load cell type annotation ctann = read.table("https://spatialecotyper.stanford.edu/inc/inc.public.vignettes.php?file=scRNAseq_demo_celltype_ann.tsv", sep = "\t",                     header = TRUE, row.names = 1) ctann = ctann[match(colnames(normdata), rownames(ctann)), 1] table(ctann) ## Load the gene expression matrix scdata = fread("https://spatialecotyper.stanford.edu/inc/inc.public.vignettes.php?file=scRNAseq_demo_counts.tsv", sep = "\t", data.table = FALSE) rownames(scdata) = scdata[, 1] ## Set the first column as row names scdata = scdata[, -1] ## Drop the first column  ## Normalize the data normdata = NormalizeData(scdata) head(normdata[, 1:5]) ##            AAACCTGCACATGACT-1 AAACCTGGTGGTCCGT-1 AAACCTGGTTTGTGTG-1 ## AL627309.1                  0                  0                  0 ## AL627309.5                  0                  0                  0 ## AP006222.2                  0                  0                  0 ##            AAACCTGTCCGATATG-1 AAACCTGTCTAACGGT-1 ## AL627309.1          0.0000000                  0 ## AL627309.5          0.2951209                  0 ## AP006222.2          0.0000000                  0 ## AC114498.1          0.0000000                  0 ## AL669831.2          0.0000000                  0 ## LINC01409           0.0000000                  0 ## Load cell type annotation ctann = read.table("https://spatialecotyper.stanford.edu/inc/inc.public.vignettes.php?file=scRNAseq_demo_celltype_ann.tsv", sep = "\t",                     header = TRUE, row.names = 1) ctann = ctann[match(colnames(normdata), rownames(ctann)), 1] table(ctann) ## title: Recovering Spatial Ecotypes from Single-Cell RNA-seq Data
text: RecoverSE function used assign single cells SEs. Users can either use default model recover predefined SEs use custom model recover newly defined SEs. Note: Cell type annotation (celltypes argument) required SE recovery using RecoverSE. default NMF models trained discovery MERSCOPE data, encompassing five cancer types: melanoma, four carcinomas. models tailored nine distinct cell types: B cells, CD4+ T cells, CD8+ T cells, NK cells, plasma cells, macrophages, dendritic cells, fibroblasts, endothelial cells. model facilitates recovery SEs single-cell datasets, allowing cell-type-specific SE analysis. SE recovery, cells query data grouped one “B”, “CD4T”, “CD8T”, “NK”, “Plasma”, “Macrophage”, “DC”, “Fibroblast”, “Endothelial”, case sensitive. cell types considered non-SE compartments. use custom models, users first develop recovery models following tutorial NMF Model Development Spatial Ecotype Recovery Single-Cell Spatial Transcriptomics Data. resulting models can used SE recovery. example model available SE_Recovery_W_list.rds. Download example models Load custom models Using custom models SE recovery specifying Ws argument. prediction, recommend retaining assignments corresponding SE-specific cell states identified Tutorial 3. Cells assigned SE-specific states grouped “NonSE,” robustly mapped corresponding SE. SE recovery output contains five columns: cell ID (CID), cell type (CellType), initial SE assignment (InitSE), final SE assignment (SE), prediction score (PredScore). default, assignments PredScore ≥ 0.6 retained high-confidence results. custom models, users encouraged determine optimal threshold based leave-one-sample-cross-validation performance.
code: sepreds <- RecoverSE(normdata, celltypes = ctann) head(sepreds) ##                                   CID   CellType InitSE    SE PredScore ## AAACCTGCACATGACT-1 AAACCTGCACATGACT-1 Macrophage   SE10   SE8 0.9958701 ## AAACCTGGTGGTCCGT-1 AAACCTGGTGGTCCGT-1          B   SE03 NonSE 0.5169725 ## AAACCTGGTTTGTGTG-1 AAACCTGGTTTGTGTG-1       CD4T   SE01   SE1 0.9999803 ## AAACCTGTCCGATATG-1 AAACCTGTCCGATATG-1 Macrophage   SE10   SE8 0.9920874 ## AAACCTGTCTAACGGT-1 AAACCTGTCTAACGGT-1          B   SE01   SE1 0.7673859 ## AAACGGGCACTTAAGC-1 AAACGGGCACTTAAGC-1       CD8T   SE03 NonSE 0.7605514 # Results:  ### InitSE: Initial SE assignment ### SE: Final SE assignment (retaining only SE-specific cell states) ### PredScore: Prediction confidence score (default threshold ≥ 0.6 for high-confidence assignments) url <- "https://spatialecotyper.stanford.edu/inc/inc.public.vignettes.php?file=SE_Recovery_W_list.rds" download.file(url, destfile = "SE_Recovery_W_list.rds", mode = "wb") Ws <- readRDS("SE_Recovery_W_list.rds") names(Ws) ## named list of W matrices ## [1] "CD4T"        "CD8T"        "DC"          "Endothelial" "Fibroblast"  ## [6] "Macrophage"  "NK"          "Plasma" head(Ws[[1]]) ## feature by SE matrix ##                  SE01      SE02      SE03      SE04      SE05      SE06 ## CD226__pos  0.4145936 0.2555579 0.3323735 0.3241147 0.2263783 0.3279133 ## CDKN1B__pos 0.5729663 0.4552065 0.5120732 0.4627805 0.4453117 0.5156023 ## CXCR4__pos  0.5865790 0.2771225 0.4093821 0.3607076 0.3589891 0.3937211 ## DUSP1__pos  0.4233067 0.3462390 0.2269798 0.1616814 0.3265539 0.2562118 ## KLF2__pos   0.5889618 0.2314586 0.3867504 0.2295206 0.2998234 0.5173246 ## ICAM2__pos  0.4593230 0.2611751 0.4427930 0.3143084 0.3679498 0.4532637 ##                  SE07      SE08      SE09      SE10      SE11 ## CD226__pos  0.2189382 0.3417897 0.2563481 0.2360192 0.2815938 ## CDKN1B__pos 0.4414750 0.4747166 0.4414508 0.3922732 0.3821411 ## CXCR4__pos  0.3751669 0.4456410 0.3023204 0.2629156 0.2729461 ## DUSP1__pos  0.3682236 0.2211759 0.1713413 0.1699067 0.1121763 ## KLF2__pos   0.3570251 0.4327206 0.1732251 0.2086729 0.2118797 ## ICAM2__pos  0.2660441 0.5494585 0.2878338 0.3711269 0.2575091 sepreds <- RecoverSE(normdata, celltypes = ctann, Ws = Ws, min.score = 0.6) head(sepreds) ##                                   CID   CellType InitSE    SE PredScore ## AAACCTGCACATGACT-1 AAACCTGCACATGACT-1 Macrophage   SE10  SE10 0.9958701 ## AAACCTGGTGGTCCGT-1 AAACCTGGTGGTCCGT-1          B   SE01 NonSE 0.0000000 ## AAACCTGGTTTGTGTG-1 AAACCTGGTTTGTGTG-1       CD4T   SE01  SE01 0.9999803 ## AAACCTGTCCGATATG-1 AAACCTGTCCGATATG-1 Macrophage   SE10  SE10 0.9920874 ## AAACCTGTCTAACGGT-1 AAACCTGTCTAACGGT-1          B   SE01 NonSE 0.0000000 ## what: Validation: co-association of SE cell states across samples
title: Recovering Spatial Ecotypes from Single-Cell RNA-seq Data
text: recovering SE-specific cell states single-cell RNA-seq data, users can validate SEs testing whether cell states belonging SE co-occur expected random chance using Coassociation function. sample, cell type, SE annotations required analysis. Note: default (test = TRUE), function evaluates significance cell state co-localization within SE returns vector p-values. analysis requires cell state names follow format “SE1_CD4T”, underscore separates SE label cell type, enabling extraction SE groupings cell state names. scRNA-seq data multiple samples required co-association analysis, demonstration, randomly partition cells 10 samples. can visualize results using CooccurrenceHeatmapView function, described Tutorial 5.
code: sepreds$Sample = paste0("S", sample(1:10, nrow(sepreds), replace = TRUE)) result = Coassociation(sepreds, Sample = "Sample", SE = "SE", CellType = "CellType", nperm = 10, test = TRUE) head(result$CoassociationIndex[, 1:5]) ##                  NonSE_B NonSE_CD4T NonSE_CD8T NonSE_Epithelial ## NonSE_B                0  0.0000000  0.0000000                0 ## NonSE_CD4T             0  1.0000000  0.3891946                0 ## NonSE_CD8T             0  0.3891946  1.0000000                0 ## NonSE_Epithelial       0  0.0000000  0.0000000                0 ## NonSE_Fibroblast       0 -0.1734636  0.5831535                0 ## NonSE_Macrophage       0  0.2449513 -0.0592797                0 ##                  NonSE_Fibroblast ## NonSE_B                 0.0000000 ## NonSE_CD4T             -0.1734636 ## NonSE_CD8T              0.5831535 ## NonSE_Epithelial        0.0000000 ## NonSE_Fibroblast        1.0000000 ## NonSE_Macrophage       -0.4134136 result$Pval ##        NonSE         SE01         SE02         SE03         SE04         SE05  ## 1.177090e-07 2.622728e-02 4.816325e-04 4.407978e-04 4.104850e-03 1.005108e-03  ##         SE06         SE07         SE08         SE09         SE10         SE11  ## 5.122919e-01 6.529556e-04 3.200077e-02 2.012668e-03 5.437996e-04 1.609121e-04 ## Set test = FALSE, the function will just return a matrix of coassociation indices. CoassociationIndex = Coassociation(sepreds, Sample = "Sample",                                     SE = "SE", CellType = "CellType",                                     nperm = 10, test = FALSE) head(CoassociationIndex[, 1:5]) ##                  NonSE_B NonSE_CD4T NonSE_CD8T NonSE_Epithelial ## NonSE_B                0  0.0000000  0.0000000                0 ## NonSE_CD4T             0  1.0000000  0.3891946                0 ## NonSE_CD8T             0  0.3891946  1.0000000                0 ## NonSE_Epithelial       0  0.0000000  0.0000000                0 ## NonSE_Fibroblast       0 -0.1734636  0.5831535                0 ## NonSE_Macrophage       0  0.2449513 -0.0592797                0 ##                  NonSE_Fibroblast ## NonSE_B                 0.0000000 ## NonSE_CD4T             -0.1734636 ## NonSE_CD8T              0.5831535 ## NonSE_Epithelial        0.0000000 ## NonSE_Fibroblast        1.0000000 ## NonSE_Macrophage       -0.4134136

what: Validation: average expression of SE markers
title: Recovering Spatial Ecotypes from Single-Cell RNA-seq Data
text: Users can also validate SEs visualizing average expression SE consensus markers across recovered SEs using AverageMarkerExpression function.  AverageMarkerExpression function can also used visualize average expression gene lists specifying genesets parameter, list gene sets.

title: Recovering Spatial Ecotypes from Single-Cell Spatial Transcriptomics Data
text: RecoverSE function used assign single cells SEs. Users can either use default model recover predefined SEs use custom model recover newly defined SEs. Note: Cell type annotation (celltypes argument) required. default NMF models trained MERSCOPE data across carcinomas melanoma. model supports following cell types: B cells, CD4+ T cells, CD8+ T cells, NK cells, plasma cells, macrophages, dendritic cells, fibroblasts, endothelial cells. cells query data grouped one “B”, “CD4T”, “CD8T”, “NK”, “Plasma”, “Macrophage”, “DC”, “Fibroblast”, “Endothelial”, case sensitive. cell types assigned NonSE. SE recovery output contains five columns: cell ID (CID), cell type (CellType), initial SE assignment (InitSE), final SE assignment (SE), prediction score (PredScore). use custom model, users first develop model following tutorial NMF Model Development Spatial Ecotype Recovery Single-Cell Spatial Transcriptomics Data. resulting model can used SE recovery. example model available SE_Recovery_W_list.rds. Download custom model Load custom model Using custom model SE recovery specifying Ws argument. prediction, recommend retaining assignments corresponding SE-specific cell states identified Tutorial 3. Cells assigned SE-specific states grouped “NonSE,” robustly mapped corresponding SE. SE recovery output contains five columns: cell ID (CID), cell type (CellType), initial SE assignment (InitSE), final SE assignment (SE), prediction score (PredScore). default, assignments PredScore ≥ 0.6 retained high-confidence results. custom models, users encouraged determine optimal threshold based leave-one-sample-cross-validation performance. Combine SE recovery results metadata

what: Validation: spatial colocalization of SE cell states
title: Recovering Spatial Ecotypes from Single-Cell Spatial Transcriptomics Data
text: recovering SE-specific cell states single-cell ST data, users can validate SEs testing whether cell states belonging SE spatially colocalized expected random chance using Colocalization function. spatial coordinates (column X Y), cell type (CellType) cell state annotations (State) required analysis. Note: default (test = TRUE), function evaluates significance cell state co-localization within SE returns vector p-values. analysis requires cell state names follow format “SE1_CD4T”, underscore separates SE label cell type, enabling extraction SE groupings cell state names. can visualize results using CooccurrenceHeatmapView function.  , small demo data, can see strong colocalization cell states within SEs, whereas SE9 shows weaker pattern, although approaches statistical significance (P < 0.1). contrast, NonSE group, serving negative control, exhibit spatial colocalization.

title: Discovering Spatial Ecotypes from a Single Spatial Transcriptomics Sample
text: tutorial, illustrate perform de novo discovery spatial ecotypes single-cell spatial transcriptomics dataset using SpatialEcoTyper. analyzing single-cell spatial transcriptomics data melanoma sample (raw data available Vizgen’s MERSCOPE FFPE Human Immuno-oncology). demo data comprises spatial expression 500 genes across 27,907 cells, categorized ten distinct cell types: B cells, CD4+ T cells, CD8+ T cells, NK cells, plasma cells, macrophages, dendritic cells (DC), fibroblasts, endothelial cells, melanoma cells. Melanoma cells excluded reduce computational time. cells grouped four spatial regions: tumor, inner margin, outer margin, stroma. tumor (tumor inner margin) stroma regions (stroma. outer margin) defined based density cancer cells, described CytoSPACE paper. inner outer margins defined regions extending 250 μm inside outside tumor boundaries, respectively. Furthermore, quantified cell’s distance tumor–stroma interface calculating shortest Euclidean distance nearest tumor region (stromal cells) stromal region (tumor cells). positive distance indicates cells located within tumor region, negative distance indicates cells located within stroma. SpatialEcoTyper requires two input data objects: Gene expression matrix: numeric matrix genes rows cells columns. Metadata: data frame containing least three columns: “X” (x-coordinate), “Y” (y-coordinate), “CellType” (cell type annotation). Row names must match column names (cell IDs) expression matrix. First load required packages vignette
code: suppressPackageStartupMessages(library(dplyr)) suppressPackageStartupMessages(library(ggplot2)) suppressPackageStartupMessages(library(parallel)) suppressPackageStartupMessages(library(Seurat)) suppressPackageStartupMessages(library(data.table)) suppressPackageStartupMessages(library(R.utils))  library(SpatialEcoTyper)

title: Discovering Spatial Ecotypes from a Single Spatial Transcriptomics Sample
code: ## Load single-cell gene expression matrix (genes × cells) url <- "https://spatialecotyper.stanford.edu/inc/inc.public.vignettes.php?file=Melanoma1_subset_counts.tsv.gz" scdata <- fread(url, sep = "\t",header = TRUE, data.table = FALSE) rownames(scdata) <- scdata[, 1] scdata <- as.matrix(scdata[, -1])  ## Normalize the gene expression data normdata <- NormalizeData(scdata)  ## Load single-cell metadata ## Required columns: "X", "Y", and "CellType" ## Row names must match the cell IDs in the expression matrix url <- "https://spatialecotyper.stanford.edu/inc/inc.public.vignettes.php?file=Melanoma1_subset_scmeta.tsv" scmeta <- read.table(url, sep = "\t", header = TRUE, row.names = 1) scmeta <- scmeta[match(colnames(scdata), rownames(scmeta)), ] head(scmeta[, c("X", "Y", "CellType")])  ## Discover spatial ecotypes using the SpatialEcoTyper se_results <- SpatialEcoTyper(normdata, scmeta,                                outprefix = "Melanoma1_subset",                               radius = 50, ncores = 2)

title: Discovering Spatial Ecotypes from a Single Spatial Transcriptomics Sample
text: SpatialEcoTyper function designed identify spatial ecotypes (SEs) single-cell spatial transcriptomics data. workflow begins defining spatial neighborhoods (SNs) regular grid constructing cell type–specific gene expression profiles (GEPs) SN. cell type, similarity network constructed, nodes represent SNs edges reflect transcriptional similarity. cell type–specific networks integrated using Similarity Network Fusion (SNF), originally developed multi-omics data integration (Wang et al., 2014). yields fused similarity network captures shared spatial transcriptomic variation across cell types, enabling identification SEs clustering. start, please check key arguments . Choosing radius parameter: optimal spatial neighborhood radius depends biological question. Larger radii capture broader regional patterns, whereas smaller radii resolve localized cellular niches. analysis, used radius 50 µm, typically includes ~10–80 neighboring cells provides practical balance granularity robustness. Choosing number variable features: nfeatures argument selected according underlying data platform. Vizgen MERSCOPE V1 (500-gene panel), used 200 highly variable features, whereas Xenium Prime data, used 300 variable features. single-cell spatial transcriptomics technologies continue evolve, number detectable genes per cell steadily increased. example, latest 10x Aera platform captures 1,000 genes per cell average. high-complexity datasets, recommend using 1,000 highly variable genes downstream analysis better capture biological variability. Limiting number cell types per spatial neighborhood: min.cts.per.region argument allows users filter spatial regions based cellular diversity requiring minimum number distinct cell types per region (default: ≥ 2). helps ensure downstream analyses focus biologically informative neighborhoods sufficient cellular heterogeneity. similarity network fusion (Step 2 SpatialEcoTyper) computationally demanding step runtime increases number spatial neighborhoods (SNs) per sample. Performance depends dataset size structure rather tissue technology. example, analysis involving 10 cell types 18,564 SNs required 436 minutes runtime reached peak memory usage 169 GB. benchmark obtained computing cluster equipped AMD EPYC 7543 processor (2.75 GHz) 256 GB RAM, using single CPU core excluding data loading time. reduce runtime, users can increase number cores via ncores parameter. However, parallelization comes cost increased memory consumption. memory resources limited, users can increase grid.size parameter, reduce number spatial neighborhoods, thereby substantially lowering memory usage improving computational efficiency.
code: se_results <- SpatialEcoTyper(normdata, scmeta,                               outprefix = "Melanoma1_subset",                               radius = 50, ncores = 2)

title: Discovering Spatial Ecotypes from a Single Spatial Transcriptomics Sample
text: outprefix specified, SpatialEcoTyper result saved RDS file named outprefix_SpatialEcoTyper_results.rds. result can loaded R using readRDS. SpatialEcoTyper result list containing two key components: Seurat object constructed fused network embedding spatial neighborhoods, enabling clustering downstream visualization SEs. Metadata, single-cell metadata SE annotations appended. Note: SpatialEcoTyper applies stringent quality control exclude low-quality spatial neighborhoods, including insufficient detected genes (min.features), cells (min.cells), insufficient cell type diversity (min.cts.per.region). regions labeled NA. Regions excluded one resolution (radius, grid.size) may retained lower resolution pass QC. practice, different resolutions minimal impact overall results. default, NA regions removed. retain cells, set dropcell = FALSE.

code: if(!"presto" %in% installed.packages()){    BiocManager::install("devtools")   devtools::install_github("immunogenomics/presto") } library("presto")  # Ensure normalized data is aligned with metadata normdata = normdata[, rownames(scmeta)]  # Perform differential expression analysis degs = lapply(unique(scmeta$CellType), function(ct){   idx = which(scmeta$CellType==ct & !is.na(scmeta$SE))   degs = wilcoxauc(normdata[, idx], scmeta$SE[idx])   degs$CellType = ct   degs }) degs = do.call(rbind, degs)  # Example: top markers for CD4 T cells degs %>% filter(CellType=="CD4T") %>%    filter(logFC>0 & padj<0.05) %>%    arrange(desc(logFC)) %>% head() ##   feature group  avgExpr     logFC statistic       auc         pval ## 1     FN1   SE0 1.806530 0.6629562 1183830.0 0.6591026 2.224246e-31 ## 2     FOS   SE7 1.925442 0.6490756 1075864.0 0.7009114 5.421664e-41 ## 3   DUSP1   SE1 1.395032 0.6396178   72771.5 0.7583841 9.937244e-06 ## 4  CTNNB1   SE5 1.354324 0.6391400  214553.5 0.7757488 1.038918e-15 ## 5     PKM   SE5 2.373492 0.5948805  214342.5 0.7749859 8.231702e-15 ## 6     FOS   SE3 1.803486 0.4922663  608225.5 0.6483962 9.791820e-15 ##           padj    pct_in  pct_out CellType ## 1 4.581948e-29  83.26446 63.97198     CD4T ## 2 2.233725e-38  96.04938 79.36675     CD4T ## 3 4.094145e-03  86.95652 64.76510     CD4T ## 4 4.280343e-13  94.02985 68.19283     CD4T ## 5 1.695731e-12 100.00000 97.31105     CD4T ## 6 8.068460e-13  93.67089 80.21728     CD4T

title: Discovering Spatial Ecotypes from a Single Spatial Transcriptomics Sample
text: example identifies SE-specific markers CD4 T cells visualizes relative expression across SEs using heatmap.
code: library(tidyr) # Identify significant DEGs sigDegs = degs %>% filter(CellType=="CD4T") %>% filter(logFC>0 & padj<0.05) # Identify genes with highest specificity per SE top_markers <- sigDegs %>% group_by(feature) %>% arrange(desc(logFC)) %>%   summarize(Highest_SE = first(group), Highest_logFC = first(logFC),              Second_logFC = nth(logFC, 2, default = 0),             Delta = Highest_logFC - Second_logFC) %>% ungroup() %>%   # Keep top 5 markers per SE based on Delta   group_by(Highest_SE) %>% arrange(desc(Delta)) %>%   slice_head(n = 5) %>% ungroup()  # Prepare logFC matrix for heatmap lfcs = degs %>% filter(CellType=="CD4T") %>%          pivot_wider(id_cols = feature, names_from = group,                     values_from = logFC) %>% as.data.frame rownames(lfcs) = lfcs$feature lfcs = lfcs[, -1] gg <- lfcs[top_markers$feature, unique(top_markers$Highest_SE), drop = FALSE]  # Scale for visualization gg = scale(t(scale(t(gg), center = FALSE))) # Plot heatmap HeatmapView(gg, breaks = c(0, 1, 1.5))

text: NMFGenerateW function used train NMF model SE deconvolution based provided SE fractions gene expression matrix. applying NMF, gene’s expression scaled mean 0 unit variance default. meet non-negativity requirement NMF, expression matrix transformed using posneg method. transformation splits expression matrix two matrices: one containing positive values containing absolute values negative values. two matrices concatenated form final training data NMF model. Note: default, NMFGenerateW selects top 2000 variable features (nfeature argument) training. training, retains 50 genes per SE (nfeature.per.se argument) model. Users can tune parameters accordingly. resulting W matrix can used infer SE abundances bulk gene expression profiles using DeconvoluteSE function. detailed documentation SE deconvolution available Inferring Spatial Ecotype Abundances Bulk Gene Expression Data.

Spatial EcoTyper framework leverages non-negative matrix factorization (NMF) core algorithm SE recovery, given robustness demonstrated effectiveness recovering carcinoma ecotypes multimodal expression data prior study. , demonstrate train model recover SEs single-cell gene expression data.

title: NMF Model Development for Spatial Ecotype Recovery from Single-Cell and Spatial Transcriptomics Data
text: NMFGenerateWList function used train cell-type specific NMF models recovering SEs. Prior NMF, gene scaled mean 0 unit variance within cell type-specific expression matrix. satisfy non-negativity requirement NMF, cell type-specific expression matrices individually processed using posneg transformation, converts expression matrix two matrices, one containing positive values containing negative values sign inverted. two matrices subsequently concatenated produce training data. NMFGenerateWList function return list feature × SE matrices, representing cell type-specific gene signature matrix rows features columns SEs. default, cell type, NMFGenerateWList balances cell numbers across samples (via Sample argument) randomly subsampling sample target size equal median number cells per sample, downsamples total 2,500 cells across samples (downsample argument), selects top 300 variable features (nfeature argument) model training. recommend adjusting parameters based characteristics dataset available computational resources. quick demonstration, train model small example dataset using simplified, built-feature selection strategy. resulting W matrices used recover SEs spatial transcriptomics single-cell RNA-seq using RecoverSE function. detailed documentation SE recovery available Recovering Spatial Ecotypes Single-Cell RNA-seq Data Recovering Spatial Ecotypes Single-Cell Spatial Transcriptomics Data. Note: obtain robust model, recommend performing multiple runs constructing ensemble. analysis (Zhang et al.), repeated training 50 times averaged resulting basis matrices generate ensemble matrix W cell type. feature selection, identified genes highest specific expression cell state run, retained consistently selected cell state half runs. NMF model training can time-consuming. improve efficiency, recommend running training jobs Slurm array jobs allocating sufficient computational resources (e.g., CPU cores) task. resulting W matrices according procedure described . example Slurm array script provided . nmf_trainings.R submit_slurm_jobs_nmf.sh jobs finish, can average resulting basis matrices. feature selection, genes showing highest specific expression cell state identified basis matrix described, genes selected cell state half repetitions retained ensemble matrix.

title: Spatial ecotype analaysis
text: Spatial EcoTyper versatile framework identifying spatially distinct multicellular communities, termed spatial ecotypes, single-cell spatial transcriptomics data. addition, provides unified methods recovery spatial ecotypes across multiple data modalities, including spatial transcriptomics, single-cell RNA-seq, bulk transcriptomic datasets.

title: Spatial ecotype analaysis
text: Spatial EcoTyper available R package, comprehensive documentation accessible https://digitalcytometry.github.io/spatialecotyper. provide eight comprehensive tutorials illustrating key functionalities Spatial EcoTyper framework: Tutorial 1: Discovering Spatial Ecotypes Single Spatial Transcriptomics Sample Tutorial 2: Discovering Conserved Spatial Ecotypes Across Multiple Spatial Transcriptomics Samples Tutorial 3: Identifying SE-Specific Cell States via Leave-One-Sample-Cross-Validation Tutorial 4: NMF Model Development Spatial Ecotype Recovery Single-Cell Spatial Transcriptomics Data Tutorial 5: Recovering Spatial Ecotypes Single-Cell Spatial Transcriptomics Data Tutorial 6: Recovering Spatial Ecotypes Single-Cell RNA-seq Data Tutorial 7: NMF Model Development Spatial Ecotype Deconvolution Bulk Gene Expression Data Tutorial 8: Inferring Spatial Ecotype Abundances Bulk Gene Expression Data Note: Spatial EcoTyper depends extensively Seurat key processes like dimensionality reduction, UMAP embedding, clustering, visualization. Initially developed using Seurat v4.3, tool thoroughly tested validated Seurat v5. Although UMAP embeddings clustering results show slight differences Seurat v4 v5, overall consistency remains strong, ensuring core biological insights preserved across versions.

Additional arguments passed nmf function.

title: Robust Clustering via NMF (non-negative matrix factorization) — nmfClustering
text: NMFfitX1 object one rank provided. list containing optimal number communities (bestK), list NMFfitX1 objects (NMFfits), ggplot object (p) displaying cophenetic coefficient across different values K.

title: Robust Clustering via NMF (non-negative matrix factorization) — nmfClustering
code: library(NMF) library(SpatialEcoTyper) mat <- matrix(rnorm(1000, 3), 20) mat[mat<0] = 0  ## Specify one rank result <- nmfClustering(mat = mat, ranks = 3, nrun.per.rank = 3) predict(result) #>  [1] 1 1 1 3 2 2 2 2 2 3 2 3 1 2 3 1 3 1 2 3 3 2 1 1 2 2 2 1 3 3 1 1 2 2 1 2 2 2 #> [39] 1 1 2 2 1 1 1 1 1 1 1 1 #> attr(,"what") #> [1] columns #> Levels: 1 2 3  ## Determine optimal ranks by testing multiple ranks result <- nmfClustering(mat = mat, ranks = 2:5, nrun.per.rank = 3) result$p  result$bestK #> [1] 2 predict(result$NMFfits[[paste0("K.", result$bestK)]]) #>  [1] 2 1 2 1 2 1 2 1 1 1 2 1 2 1 1 2 1 1 1 1 1 1 2 2 1 2 1 2 1 1 1 2 2 2 2 1 1 1 #> [39] 2 2 1 1 2 1 1 1 2 2 2 2 #> attr(,"what") #> [1] columns #> Levels: 1 2
